text:
COLON, SIGMOID, BIOPSY

Clinical History: 58-year-old male presenting for screening colonoscopy. No prior polyp history.

Macroscopic Description: Received in formalin, one fragment of tan-pink tissue measuring 0.3 x 0.2 x 0.1 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show colonic mucosa with preserved crypt architecture. There is no increase in lamina propria cellularity. Goblet cells are numerous and well-formed. No dysplasia, no granulomata, no significant inflammation, and no evidence of malignancy.

Final Diagnosis: Colonic mucosa, within normal limits.

STOMACH, ANTRUM, BIOPSY

Clinical History: 44-year-old female with epigastric pain and dyspepsia. Endoscopy reveals erythematous antral mucosa.

Macroscopic Description: Two fragments of tan-pink gastric mucosa, each measuring approximately 0.4 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show gastric antral mucosa with a mild to moderate chronic active inflammatory infiltrate in the lamina propria. Numerous curved bacilli consistent with Helicobacter pylori are identified overlying the surface epithelium. Intestinal metaplasia is not identified. No dysplasia or malignancy.

Final Diagnosis:
1. Gastric antral mucosa with chronic active gastritis.
2. Helicobacter pylori organisms identified (see note).

Note: Recommend treatment per clinical protocol.

COLON, TRANSVERSE, POLYPECTOMY

Clinical History: 63-year-old female, surveillance colonoscopy following prior adenoma resection.

Macroscopic Description: One polypoid fragment of tan-pink tissue measuring 0.8 x 0.6 x 0.4 cm. Submitted entirely.

Microscopic Description: Sections show a tubular adenoma with low-grade dysplasia. The crypts are elongated with pseudostratified nuclei showing mild nuclear enlargement and hyperchromasia. Mitotic figures are present but not atypical. No high-grade dysplasia. No evidence of invasive carcinoma. Excision appears complete.

Final Diagnosis: Colon, transverse, polypectomy — Tubular adenoma with low-grade dysplasia. Margins negative.

SIGMOID COLON RESECTION

Clinical History: 71-year-old male presenting with rectal bleeding and change in bowel habits. CT scan demonstrated a sigmoid mass with no evidence of distant metastases.

Macroscopic Description: Sigmoid colon segment measuring 22 cm in length. An ulcerated, circumferential mass measuring 4.5 x 4.0 cm is identified 8 cm from the distal resection margin. Cut surface shows grey-white tumor penetrating through the bowel wall into the pericolic fat.

Microscopic Description: Sections confirm a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon (Grade 2). The tumor infiltrates through the muscularis propria into the pericolic adipose tissue (pT3). Lymphovascular invasion is identified. Perineural invasion is present focally. All surgical margins are free of tumor. Eleven lymph nodes identified, two of which contain metastatic adenocarcinoma (2/11).

Final Diagnosis:
1. Sigmoid colon: Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, pT3 N1b Mx.
2. Lymphovascular and perineural invasion present.
3. Two of eleven lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (2/11).
4. All resection margins negative.

DUODENUM, SECOND PORTION, BIOPSY

Clinical History: 29-year-old female with iron deficiency anemia and diarrhea. Serology positive for anti-tTG IgA antibodies. Endoscopy shows scalloping of duodenal folds.

Macroscopic Description: Four fragments of tan-pink duodenal mucosa, each 0.2–0.4 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show duodenal mucosa with marked villous blunting and crypt hyperplasia. Intraepithelial lymphocytosis is prominent (>25 IELs per 100 enterocytes). The lamina propria shows a moderate increase in plasma cells and lymphocytes. Goblet cells are reduced. Features are consistent with Marsh grade 3b celiac disease.

Final Diagnosis: Duodenal mucosa with villous atrophy and intraepithelial lymphocytosis, consistent with celiac disease (Marsh grade 3b). Clinical and serologic correlation recommended.

COLON, ASCENDING, POLYPECTOMY

Clinical History: 67-year-old male, routine surveillance colonoscopy. 5 mm sessile polyp in ascending colon.

Macroscopic Description: Single fragment of tan-pink polypoid tissue measuring 0.5 x 0.4 x 0.3 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show a hyperplastic polyp. The crypts are elongated with serrated luminal architecture in the upper portions. The epithelium shows mature goblet cells with no dysplasia. There is no evidence of sessile serrated lesion morphology — the crypts lack basal dilation or horizontal growth. No malignancy.

Final Diagnosis: Colon, ascending, polypectomy — Hyperplastic polyp. No dysplasia identified.

COLON, CECUM, BIOPSY

Clinical History: 52-year-old female with longstanding Crohn's disease, presenting with exacerbation of right lower quadrant pain.

Macroscopic Description: Two fragments of tan-pink colonic mucosa, 0.5 and 0.3 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show colonic mucosa with a focally active chronic inflammatory infiltrate. There is architectural distortion with irregular crypt branching and crypt dropout. Non-caseating granulomata are identified in the lamina propria. Paneth cell metaplasia is present. No dysplasia. No malignancy.

Final Diagnosis:
1. Cecal mucosa with chronic active colitis, architectural distortion, and granulomata.
2. Features consistent with Crohn's disease involvement.
3. No dysplasia identified.

STOMACH, BODY, BIOPSY

Clinical History: 61-year-old male with incidental gastric submucosal nodule detected on upper endoscopy. Endoscopic ultrasound suggests a hypoechoic lesion arising from the muscularis propria.

Macroscopic Description: Core biopsy fragments of tan-grey tissue, aggregate 1.1 cm.

Microscopic Description: Sections show a cellular spindle cell neoplasm with fascicular growth pattern. Cells have elongate nuclei with mild atypia and a low mitotic rate (< 1 per 50 HPF). No necrosis. Immunohistochemical studies show diffuse strong positivity for CD117 (c-Kit) and DOG1. CD34 focally positive. S100 and SMA negative. Ki67 proliferation index approximately 2%.

Final Diagnosis: Gastric body biopsy — Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), low mitotic rate. See comment.

Comment: Based on size (radiologically estimated 2.1 cm) and mitotic rate, this falls into the very low risk category per AFIP criteria. Recommend oncology referral.
